did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a fine-tuned ResNet-50 architecture, to perform multi-label skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and comparison of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a fine-tuned ResNet-50 architecture for multi-label skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology (data preprocessing, augmentation, model training) on the HAM10000 dataset and reports new quantitative results (validation accuracies of 98.18% and 93.25%). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICAECT 2026), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Automated Deep Learning Framework for Multi-Label Skin Cancer Classification Using Balanced Dermatoscopic Data
journal: 2026 Sixth International Conference on Advances in Electrical, Computing, Communications and Sustainable Technologies (ICAECT)
brief_summary: This study developed and compared a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a fine-tuned ResNet-50 architecture for multi-label skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic dataset. The dataset, containing seven classes of skin lesions, was balanced and augmented to address class imbalance and improve model generalization. The models were evaluated based on validation accuracy, with the custom CNN achieving 98.18% and the ResNet-50 achieving 93.25%. The results indicate that the simpler, custom-designed CNN outperformed the deeper ResNet-50 model in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency for this specific task. The authors conclude that tailored, simpler networks can be more effective than large pre-trained models for specialized medical imaging applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-label skin cancer classification.

all_task_types.target: Seven different classes of dermatoscopic skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesion, Dermatofibroma) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: Multi-label skin cancer classification using a custom CNN and fine-tuned ResNet-50. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (98.18% for CNN, 93.25% for ResNet-50), precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermatoscopic dataset containing images of skin lesions collected from various sources. The study utilized this dataset, which contains over 10,000 images representing 7 different classes of lesions. The dataset was balanced and augmented to address class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training both the custom CNN and fine-tuned ResNet-50 models after balancing and augmentation.
use.validation: Used for calculating model performance metrics (validation accuracy).
use.other: Used for model evaluation and comparison.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using validation accuracy (98.18% for CNN vs. 93.25% for ResNet-50) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The custom CNN achieved higher validation accuracy than the fine-tuned ResNet-50. The paper notes that the simpler CNN was more efficient and effective for this specific task, while ResNet-50's pre-trained features from ImageNet did not correlate as well with dermatoscopic images. Both models were evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, but only validation accuracy values are explicitly reported in the text.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and fine-tuned ResNet-50
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet-50 base model pretrained on ImageNet dataset; Custom CNN trained from scratch

custom_cnn.regularization: Dropout layer with ratio 0.5 after the last pooling layer

resnet50.structure: 50-layer deep residual network with shortcut connections
resnet50.pretrained_base: ImageNet pretrained base model with all convolutional-based blocks frozen initially
resnet50.classification_head: Global Average pooling, Dropout layer (0.3), two Dense layers, Softmax activation head
resnet50.fine_tuning: Final two convolutional blocks unfrozen and fine-tuned with extremely low learning rate

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: Softmax layer delivering probabilities for each type of disease
classification_or_output_head.resnet50: Softmax activation head classifying the probability of each type of skin disease

preprocessing_techniques: Elimination of low-quality samples from the database
Data augmentation including random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips, random small translations, and shearing

The dataset was balanced to address class imbalance, specifically noting that the Nevoc class contained the mass of images while other classes had fewer examples. Augmentation was employed to provide a balanced number of samples in each class.

conclusions: The custom-built Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved a validation accuracy of 98.18%, outperforming the fine-tuned ResNet-50 architecture, which achieved 93.25% accuracy.
A simpler, task-specific CNN designed from scratch for dermatoscopic images can yield superior classification results compared to deeper, generic pre-trained networks like ResNet-50.

Pre-trained models like ResNet-50, which rely on features learned from natural image datasets (ImageNet), may not correlate as effectively with the specific visual patterns found in medical dermatoscopic images.
The custom CNN demonstrated greater computational efficiency and lower processing time requirements, making it more suitable for practical, real-life clinical scenarios.

Tailoring network architecture specifically to the domain (dermatoscopic imaging) allows the model to learn important visual patterns more effectively without overfitting, especially when dataset size is limited.

Deep learning frameworks have significant potential to assist dermatologists by providing quicker, more reliable, and less subjective diagnoses, thereby reducing human error and improving early detection rates.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevus is overrepresented) and potential selection bias from specific dermatological centers; results may not generalize to other populations or clinical settings without external validation.

The custom CNN achieved 98.18% validation accuracy while ResNet-50 achieved 93.25%; however, the paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to confirm that this difference is not due to chance.

The paper does not report a separate test set evaluation; performance metrics are based on validation accuracy, raising concerns about potential overfitting or data leakage if the validation split was not strictly held out.

The conclusion that simpler networks outperform deeper pre-trained models is specific to this dataset and task; it contradicts broader literature where large pre-trained models often dominate, suggesting the finding may be dataset-specific rather than a universal principle.

The paper mentions using 'penalties on the loss functions' to handle class imbalance in the methodology description but does not explicitly detail the specific loss function variant (e.g., focal loss, weighted cross-entropy) or hyperparameters used in the results.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICAECT 2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to high-impact medical AI journals.

The authors claim the custom CNN is more suitable for 'real-life clinical scenarios' due to computational efficiency, but no latency, inference time, or hardware deployment metrics are provided to support this claim.
